attributes.stem:
A 94-year-old woman is brought to see her General Practitioner by her daughter who is concerned about a reddened scaly area on her mother's left forearm. This has been present for at least three months. Her mother has a medical history of dementia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and osteoarthritis. Her medications include metformin, amlodipine, and paracetamol.

On examination, she appears frail but in no distress. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Skin examination reveals a well-demarcated, erythematous, scaly plaque measuring 3 x 3 cm on the dorsal aspect of her left forearm (Image). A punch biopsy of the lesion reveals squamous cell carcinoma in situ (Bowen's disease). The patient's family expresses a preference to avoid surgery if possible, given her age and comorbidities. They have also found it difficult to organise getting her to this appointment as she is reluctant to leave her nursing home.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Surgical excision and split skin graft
attributes.explanation:
In this case, topical 5-fluorouracil is the most appropriate treatment option. Bowen's disease, also known as squamous cell carcinoma in situ, is a precancerous condition that can progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma if left untreated. The choice of treatment depends on various factors, including the size and location of the lesion, the patient's age, overall health status, and preferences.

For this 94-year-old patient with dementia, multiple comorbidities, and a family preference to avoid surgery, a non-invasive treatment like topical 5-fluorouracil is ideal. It is effective in treating Bowen's disease, with cure rates of 80-90%. The medication is applied twice daily for 3-8 weeks, depending on the response. It causes local inflammation and destruction of the abnormal cells while sparing normal skin. This option is particularly suitable for a patient who is reluctant to leave her nursing home, as it can be easily administered by care staff.

Cryotherapy is another non-surgical option for Bowen's disease. However, it is generally more suitable for smaller lesions (less than 2 cm) and may require multiple treatments. Given the size of this patient's lesion (3 x 3 cm), cryotherapy might not be as effective as topical 5-fluorouracil. Additionally, multiple visits for treatment would be challenging for this patient who is reluctant to leave her nursing home.

Mohs micrographic surgery is a specialised surgical technique that offers high cure rates and tissue conservation. However, it is an invasive procedure that requires multiple stages and can be time-consuming. Given the patient's advanced age, dementia, and the family's preference to avoid surgery, this option is not suitable in this case.

Photodynamic therapy is an effective treatment for Bowen's disease, especially for larger or multiple lesions. However, it requires specialised equipment and expertise, which may not be readily available in all settings. It also typically involves multiple visits to the clinic, which would be challenging for this elderly patient who is reluctant to leave her nursing home.

Surgical excision is considered the gold standard treatment for Bowen's disease, offering high cure rates and allowing histological confirmation of complete removal. However, given the patient's advanced age, dementia, multiple comorbidities, and the family's preference to avoid surgery, this option is less suitable in this case. The stress of surgery and wound healing could be significant for a frail 94-year-old patient.

In conclusion, topical 5-fluorouracil offers an effective, non-invasive treatment option that aligns with the patient's and family's preferences while addressing the Bowen's disease lesion. It provides a good balance between efficacy and minimising potential complications in this high-risk patient, and can be easily administered in the nursing home setting.
learningPoints: Topical 5-fluorouracil is an effective non-surgical treatment for Bowen's disease, particularly in elderly patients with comorbidities.
Bowen's disease (squamous cell carcinoma in situ) presents as a slow-growing, scaly, erythematous plaque on sun-exposed areas.
Treatment choice for Bowen's disease depends on factors such as lesion size, location, patient age, and comorbidities.
references: Clarke P. Nonmelanoma skin cancers treatment options. Australian Family Physician. 2012;41(7). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2012/july/nonmelanoma-skin-cancers-treatment-options
The Australiasian College of Dermatologists. Bowen's Disease. 2024. Available at: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/bowens-disease/

Morley GL, Matthews JH, Verpetinske I, Thom GA. A Comparative Study Examining the Management of Bowen's Disease in the United Kingdom and Australia. Dermatol Res Pract. 2015. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4579309/
